days.theme: Awakening to the Cause of Husayn (AS)

dua.arabic: إِنَّا لِلَّهِ وَإِنَّا إِلَيْهِ رَاجِعُونَ، رِضَا اللَّهِ رِضَانَا أَهْلَ الْبَيْتِ، نَصْبِرُ عَلَىٰ بَلَائِهِ وَيُوَفِّينَا أُجُورَ الصَّابِرِينَ
dua.transliteration: Inna lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un. Rida-llahi ridana ahla-l-bayt, nasbiru 'ala bala'ihi wa yuwaffina ujura-s-sabirin
dua.english: Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return. The pleasure of Allah is our pleasure, the People of the House; we are patient through His trial, and He will pay us in full the reward of the patient.
dua.source: Words of Imam al-Husayn (AS) on departing for Karbala — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus); the verse is Quran 2:156

verses.relevanceNote: 'Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we return' — the istirja' the believer turns to in calamity; the gateway phrase of all azadari for Imam al-Husayn (AS).
'O reassured soul, return to your Lord' — the verse Shia tradition reads upon Imam al-Husayn (AS); to awaken to his cause is to begin the journey of that returning soul.
'Seek help through patience and prayer' — the posture with which one should enter the ten days of mourning.

days.tafsirFocus: Muharram opens not with celebration but with a turning of the heart. The istirja' of 2:156 is not mere resignation; in the tafsir of grief it is the soul recognising that everything, even the beloved Imam (AS), belongs to Allah and returns to Him. Husayn (AS) himself spoke these words as he set out, accepting Allah's decree with sabr. To 'awaken' to his cause is to let his sacrifice pierce a heart that had grown comfortable, and to enter these days seeking Allah through patience and prayer.
days.reflection: Before the mourning begins, sit in silence. Has the tragedy of Husayn (AS) become a familiar story you no longer feel? Ask Allah to reopen this wound in your heart.

days.theme: Truth Over Comfort — The Refusal to Pledge to Yazid

dua.arabic: إِنَّا أَهْلُ بَيْتِ النُّبُوَّةِ، وَمَعْدِنُ الرِّسَالَةِ، وَمُخْتَلَفُ الْمَلَائِكَةِ، وَيَزِيدُ رَجُلٌ فَاسِقٌ، وَمِثْلِي لَا يُبَايِعُ مِثْلَهُ
dua.transliteration: Inna ahlu bayti-n-nubuwwah, wa ma'dinu-r-risalah, wa mukhtalafu-l-mala'ikah, wa Yazidu rajulun fasiq, wa mithli la yubayi'u mithlah
dua.english: We are the household of prophethood, the substance of the message, and those whom the angels visit; and Yazid is a corrupt man — a man like me does not pledge allegiance to a man like him.
dua.source: Reply of Imam al-Husayn (AS) to al-Walid ibn Utba in Medina — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus); Maqtal al-Husayn (al-Muqarram)

verses.relevanceNote: 'Do not mix truth with falsehood, nor conceal the truth while you know it' — Husayn (AS) would not lend a false legitimacy to Yazid by his silence or his hand.
'Do not incline toward those who do wrong, lest the Fire touch you' — the refusal of bay'ah was obedience to this command, even at the cost of life.
If wealth, kin and dwellings are more beloved than Allah and His cause, await His decree — Husayn (AS) chose Allah's cause over the safety of comfort.

days.tafsirFocus: When al-Walid summoned him to pledge allegiance, Imam al-Husayn (AS) did not weigh his life against the truth. The Quran forbids cloaking falsehood in the garment of truth (2:42) and forbids leaning toward oppressors (11:113). Yazid's rule was open corruption; to pledge to it was to conceal a truth that the household of prophethood was created to guard. The lens here is the cost of honesty: faith sometimes demands that comfort, safety and even life be set beneath the truth.
days.reflection: Where in your own life do you stay silent for comfort when the truth needs a voice? Husayn (AS) refused with everything to lose. What do you refuse to risk?

days.theme: Migration for Principle — Medina to Mecca to Iraq

dua.arabic: إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ، رَبِّ نَجِّنِي مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ
dua.transliteration: Inni dhahibun ila rabbi sayahdin. Rabbi najjini mina-l-qawmi-z-zalimin
dua.english: Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me. My Lord, deliver me from the wrongdoing people.
dua.source: Quran 37:99 (words of Prophet Ibrahim AS on his hijrah) and 28:21 (words of Prophet Musa AS leaving Egypt) — supplications of departure for the sake of Allah

verses.relevanceNote: 'My Lord, deliver me from the wrongdoing people' — the prayer of Musa (AS) leaving Egypt; Husayn (AS) likewise left Medina for Mecca then Iraq rather than submit to wrong.
'Indeed I am going to my Lord; He will guide me' — Ibrahim's (AS) words as he migrated for his Lord; the spirit of Husayn's (AS) journey toward Allah's decree.
'Indeed I will emigrate to my Lord' — migration in the Quran is movement for principle, not escape; the model for Husayn's (AS) journey.

days.tafsirFocus: Imam al-Husayn (AS) left Medina near the end of Rajab, reached Mecca, and then set out for Iraq rather than give his hand to Yazid. The Quran frames the migrations of Ibrahim and Musa (AS) not as flight but as deliberate movement toward Allah and away from oppression (37:99, 28:21, 29:26). Husayn's (AS) journey is read in the same light: every stage was a refusal to let principle be buried for safety. The tafsir lens is hijrah as an act of the heart before it is a movement of the body.
days.reflection: Husayn (AS) left the city of his grandfather ﷺ rather than betray a principle. What comfort or place would you have to leave to keep your faith whole?

days.theme: Loyalty & True Companionship — The Ansar of Husayn (AS)

dua.arabic: السَّلَامُ عَلَيْكُمْ يَا أَنْصَارَ اللَّهِ وَأَنْصَارَ رَسُولِهِ وَأَنْصَارَ عَلِيِّ بْنِ أَبِي طَالِبٍ، وَأَنْصَارَ الْحَسَنِ، وَأَنْصَارَ الْحُسَيْنِ صَلَوَاتُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِمْ
dua.transliteration: As-salamu 'alaykum ya ansara-llahi wa ansara rasulih, wa ansara 'Aliyyi-bni Abi Talib, wa ansara-l-Hasan, wa ansara-l-Husayn salawatu-llahi 'alayhim
dua.english: Peace be upon you, O helpers of Allah and helpers of His Messenger, and helpers of Ali ibn Abi Talib, and helpers of al-Hasan, and helpers of al-Husayn — Allah's blessings be upon them.
dua.source: Ziyarat al-Shuhada (the martyrs of Karbala) — Mafatih al-Jinan

verses.relevanceNote: 'Among the believers are men true to what they pledged to Allah' — recited in Ziyarat for the loyal companions who fulfilled their covenant unto death at Karbala.
'Those with him are firm against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves' — a portrait of Husayn's (AS) Ansar on the night and morning of Ashura.
'Allah has purchased from the believers their lives in return for Paradise' — the companions of Husayn (AS) gave their souls in that very transaction.

days.tafsirFocus: The companions of Imam al-Husayn (AS) are remembered in Ziyarat with the words of 33:23 — believers true to what they pledged to Allah. Few in number, they would not abandon the grandson of the Prophet ﷺ. The Quran's image of the firm and merciful believers (48:29) and of the soul sold to Allah for Paradise (9:111) is fulfilled in them. The lens here is loyalty: true companionship is proven not in ease but at the moment when leaving would have been easy and forgivable, and they stayed.
days.reflection: Husayn's (AS) companions could have walked away in the dark and been blameless. They chose him. To whom, or to what truth, is your loyalty tested when leaving would be easy?

days.theme: Patience Under Trial — The Water Cut Off

dua.arabic: صَبْرًا عَلَىٰ قَضَائِكَ يَا رَبِّ، لَا إِلَٰهَ سِوَاكَ، يَا غِيَاثَ الْمُسْتَغِيثِينَ، مَا لِي رَبٌّ سِوَاكَ، وَلَا مَعْبُودَ غَيْرُكَ
dua.transliteration: Sabran 'ala qada'ika ya rabb, la ilaha siwak, ya ghiyatha-l-mustaghithin, ma li rabbun siwak, wa la ma'buda ghayruk
dua.english: Patience in the face of Your decree, O my Lord; there is no god but You, O Helper of those who cry for help. I have no Lord but You, and none worshipped besides You.
dua.source: Verbatim supplication of Imam al-Husayn (AS) on the day of Ashura — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus); Maqtal al-Husayn (al-Muqarram, p. 283); Bihar al-Anwar (al-Majlisi)

verses.relevanceNote: 'We will surely test you with fear, hunger and loss... and give glad tidings to the patient' — the camp of Husayn (AS) endured exactly this when the water was cut off.
'Seek help through patience and prayer; indeed Allah is with the patient' — the inner provision of Karbala when every outer provision, even water, was denied.
'The patient will be given their reward without measure' — the immeasurable station earned through the thirst borne for Allah at Karbala.

days.tafsirFocus: From the seventh of Muharram, Umar ibn Sa'd cut the water of the Euphrates from the camp of Imam al-Husayn (AS); the children and the companions thirsted. The Quran promises that the believers will be tested with fear, hunger and loss, and that glad tidings belong to the patient (2:155). Sabr in the Quran is not passive endurance but an active turning to Allah while the trial burns (2:153). The lens is patience as worship: the thirst of Karbala was borne not in despair but in submission, and its reward is 'without measure' (39:10).
days.reflection: Recall the thirst of the children of Husayn (AS) before you drink today. When trial withholds what you need, do you turn to Allah with sabr, or only with complaint?

days.themeArabic: العِزَّةُ لَا الذِّلَّة — هَيْهَاتَ مِنَّا الذِّلَّة

dua.arabic: وَهَيْهَاتَ مِنَّا الذِّلَّةُ، يَأْبَى اللَّهُ لَنَا ذَٰلِكَ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ، وَحُجُورٌ طَابَتْ وَطَهُرَتْ، وَأُنُوفٌ حَمِيَّةٌ، وَنُفُوسٌ أَبِيَّةٌ، مِنْ أَنْ تُؤْثِرَ طَاعَةَ اللِّئَامِ عَلَىٰ مَصَارِعِ الْكِرَامِ
dua.transliteration: Wa hayhata minna-dh-dhillah, ya'ba-llahu lana dhalika wa rasuluhu wa-l-mu'minun, wa hujurun tabat wa tahurat, wa anufun hamiyyah, wa nufusun abiyyah, min an tu'thira ta'ata-l-li'ami 'ala masari'i-l-kiram
dua.english: Far, far from us is humiliation. Allah refuses that for us, and His Messenger, and the believers, and laps that were pure and chaste, and proud souls that disdain to prefer the obedience of the vile over the noble death of the honourable.
dua.source: Ashura sermon of Imam al-Husayn (AS) — Tuhaf al-Uqul (Ibn Shu'ba al-Harrani); also al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus)

verses.relevanceNote: 'To Allah belongs all honour, and to His Messenger and the believers' — the very source of the dignity Husayn (AS) refused to surrender to Yazid.
'Do not lose heart nor grieve, for you are the higher if you are believers' — the elevation of the believer that humiliation cannot reach.
Honour belongs entirely to Allah — so it cannot be sought from, nor lost to, a tyrant like Yazid.

days.tafsirFocus: On the day of Ashura, Imam al-Husayn (AS) declared 'hayhāt minna-dh-dhilla' — humiliation is far from us. The Quran roots all 'izza, all honour, in Allah, His Messenger and the believers (63:8), and tells the believers they are the higher ones (3:139). Honour sought from a tyrant is no honour at all (4:139). The lens is the Quranic meaning of dignity: it is not pride of self but refusal to bow the soul to anyone but Allah, even when the cost is the 'noble death of the honourable'.
days.reflection: Husayn (AS) would die rather than live one breath in humiliation before falsehood. What small humiliations of conscience do you accept for an easier life?

days.theme: Sacrifice — The Offering of Sons and Brothers

dua.arabic: قَتَلَ اللَّهُ قَوْمًا قَتَلُوكَ، مَا أَجْرَأَهُمْ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَعَلَىٰ انْتِهَاكِ حُرْمَةِ الرَّسُولِ، عَلَى الدُّنْيَا بَعْدَكَ الْعَفَاءُ
dua.transliteration: Qatala-llahu qawman qataluk, ma ajra'ahum 'ala-llahi wa 'ala-ntihaki hurmati-r-rasul, 'ala-d-dunya ba'daka-l-'afa'
dua.english: May Allah slay a people who slew you. How bold they were against Allah and against the violation of the sanctity of the Messenger! Upon the world, after you, be desolation.
dua.source: Verbatim words of Imam al-Husayn (AS) at the martyrdom of his son Ali al-Akbar — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus, p. 67); also Bihar al-Anwar (al-Majlisi, vol. 45, p. 65) and al-Irshad (Shaykh al-Mufid, vol. 2, p. 106)

verses.relevanceNote: 'And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice' — read in Shia tradition as foreshadowing the supreme sacrifice fulfilled by Husayn (AS) at Karbala.
'Do not say of those slain in the way of Allah that they are dead; rather they are alive' — the sons and brothers offered at Karbala live with their Lord.
'Those slain in the way of Allah are alive with their Lord, receiving provision' — the station of Ali al-Akbar, al-Abbas and the household given for Husayn (AS).

days.tafsirFocus: Imam al-Husayn (AS) offered his sons, his brothers and his companions one by one — Ali al-Akbar, the infant Ali al-Asghar, his brother al-Abbas. The Quran calls Ibrahim's offering a 'great sacrifice' (37:107); the maqtal tradition sees its true fulfilment at Karbala. Yet the Quran insists the slain in Allah's way are not dead but living, provided for by their Lord (2:154, 3:169). The lens is sacrifice as transaction with Allah: what was given at Karbala was not lost but transformed into eternal life.
days.reflection: Husayn (AS) buried his own sons for the sake of Allah's religion. Hold that grief for a moment. What do you cling to that you would not give for Allah?

days.theme: Steadfastness of the Women — Sayyida Zaynab (AS)
days.themeArabic: ثَبَاتُ النِّسَاء — السَّيِّدَةُ زَيْنَبُ عَلَيْهَا السَّلام

dua.arabic: مَا رَأَيْتُ إِلَّا جَمِيلًا، هَٰؤُلَاءِ قَوْمٌ كَتَبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَتْلَ فَبَرَزُوا إِلَىٰ مَضَاجِعِهِمْ، وَسَيَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ بَيْنَكَ وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتُحَاجُّ وَتُخَاصَمُ
dua.transliteration: Ma ra'aytu illa jamila. Ha'ula'i qawmun kataba-llahu 'alayhimu-l-qatla fa-barazu ila madaji'ihim, wa sa-yajma'u-llahu baynaka wa baynahum fa-tuhajju wa tukhasam
dua.english: I saw nothing but beauty. These were a people for whom Allah had decreed martyrdom, so they went forth to their resting places; and Allah will gather you and them, and you will be questioned and disputed against.
dua.source: Reply of Sayyida Zaynab (AS) to Yazid in his court — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus); Nafasul Mahmoom (Shaykh Abbas Qummi)

verses.relevanceNote: 'Sufficient for us is Allah, and He is the best Disposer of affairs' — the unbroken faith of Sayyida Zaynab (AS) before Ibn Ziyad and Yazid.
'Allah keeps firm those who believe with the firm word' — the divinely-given steadfastness that held Zaynab (AS) upright through Karbala, Kufa and Sham.
'Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured' — the salutation of the angels, fitting for the patience of the women of Karbala.

days.tafsirFocus: After the slaughter, the women and children were taken captive to Kufa and Sham. When Yazid mocked her, Sayyida Zaynab (AS) answered, 'I saw nothing but beauty', and delivered a sermon that shook his court. The Quran's words 'sufficient for us is Allah' (3:173) and 'Allah keeps firm those who believe' (14:27) describe her exactly: a faith that captivity could not bend. The lens is steadfastness — the women of Karbala carried the message of Husayn (AS) when the men had been martyred, and their patience earns the angels' greeting of peace (13:24).
days.reflection: Zaynab (AS) stood before the killer of her brother and called the decree of Allah beautiful. When grief and injustice strike together, can your faith still say 'sufficient for us is Allah'?

days.theme: The Night of Ashura — Worship and Resolve

dua.arabic: أُثْنِي عَلَى اللَّهِ أَحْسَنَ الثَّنَاءِ، أَمَّا بَعْدُ، فَإِنِّي لَا أَعْلَمُ أَصْحَابًا أَوْفَىٰ وَلَا خَيْرًا مِنْ أَصْحَابِي، وَلَا أَهْلَ بَيْتٍ أَبَرَّ وَلَا أَوْصَلَ مِنْ أَهْلِ بَيْتِي، فَجَزَاكُمُ اللَّهُ عَنِّي خَيْرًا
dua.transliteration: Uthni 'ala-llahi ahsana-th-thana'. Amma ba'd, fa-inni la a'lamu as-haban awfa wa la khayran min as-habi, wa la ahla baytin abarra wa la awsala min ahli bayti, fa-jazakumu-llahu 'anni khayra
dua.english: I praise Allah with the most beautiful praise. As to what follows: I know of no companions more loyal or better than my companions, nor of a household more dutiful or more devoted than my household; may Allah reward you well on my behalf.
dua.source: Address of Imam al-Husayn (AS) to his companions on the night of Ashura — al-Lohoof (Sayyid ibn Tawus)

verses.relevanceNote: 'They used to sleep but little of the night' — the night of Ashura, when Husayn's (AS) camp spent the dark hours in prayer like the buzzing of bees.
'Those who pass the night before their Lord prostrating and standing' — the very portrait of the companions on Layla Ashura.
'Remember the name of your Lord and devote yourself to Him completely' — the resolve with which they prepared to meet Allah at dawn.

days.tafsirFocus: On the eve of Ashura, Imam al-Husayn (AS) gathered his people, praised Allah, lifted his obligation from them and bade them leave under the cover of night — and not one departed. They then passed the night in worship, the sound of their ruku' and sujud like the humming of bees. The Quran praises those who sleep little of the night and stand before their Lord (51:17, 25:64) and calls the believer to devote himself wholly to Allah (73:8). The lens is worship as the source of resolve: the steadfastness of Ashura morning was forged in the prayers of Ashura night.
days.reflection: They knew they would die at dawn, and they spent the night in prayer, not in fear. If you knew this were your last night, how would you spend it — and what stops you tonight?

days.theme: Ashura — The Supreme Sacrifice and Its Eternal Message

dua.arabic: السَّلَامُ عَلَيْكَ يَا أَبَا عَبْدِ اللَّهِ وَعَلَى الْأَرْوَاحِ الَّتِي حَلَّتْ بِفِنَائِكَ، اللَّهُمَّ ارْزُقْنِي شَفَاعَةَ الْحُسَيْنِ يَوْمَ الْوُرُودِ، وَثَبِّتْ لِي قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَكَ مَعَ الْحُسَيْنِ وَأَصْحَابِ الْحُسَيْنِ الَّذِينَ بَذَلُوا مُهَجَهُمْ دُونَ الْحُسَيْنِ عَلَيْهِ السَّلَامُ
dua.transliteration: As-salamu 'alayka ya Aba 'Abdillah wa 'ala-l-arwahi-llati hallat bi-fina'ik. Allahumma-rzuqni shafa'ata-l-Husayni yawma-l-wurud, wa thabbit li qadama sidqin 'indaka ma'a-l-Husayni wa as-habi-l-Husayni-lladhina badhalu muhajahum duna-l-Husayni 'alayhi-s-salam
dua.english: Peace be upon you, O Aba Abdillah, and upon the souls that gathered at your courtyard. O Allah, grant me the intercession of al-Husayn on the Day of Arrival, and grant me a truthful standing with You alongside al-Husayn and the companions of al-Husayn — those who gave their lifeblood for al-Husayn, peace be upon him.
dua.source: Ziyarat Ashura (opening salutation and concluding supplication) — Mafatih al-Jinan (Shaykh Abbas Qummi)

verses.relevanceNote: 'O reassured soul, return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing' — the verses Shia tradition addresses to Imam al-Husayn (AS) as he fell on the sands of Karbala.
'Do not think those slain in the way of Allah are dead; rather they live with their Lord, receiving provision' — the eternal life of the martyr of Ashura.
Allah defends the believers and will surely aid those who aid His cause — Husayn (AS) gave everything so that Allah's religion would not be erased; the message that outlives Yazid.

days.tafsirFocus: On the tenth of Muharram, after his sons, brothers and companions, Imam al-Husayn (AS) gave himself — the supreme sacrifice. Shia tradition reads over him the verses of the reassured soul returning to its Lord (89:27-30). The Quran's truth that the martyr is not dead but living and provided for (3:169) is realised in him most of all, and his stand preserved Allah's religion when it was nearest to being effaced (22:40). The lens is the eternity of Ashura: a defeat in the field that became, by the Quran's measure, the most enduring victory — every day Ashura, every land Karbala.
days.reflection: Today the heart should break. Husayn (AS) gave his life so the religion of his grandfather ﷺ would live. Weep for him, and then ask: what in your life is now demanded for that same truth?
